name: Tonga
description: The Tonga are Zambia's most disrupted people in the 20th century — the Kariba Dam (1958) forcibly displaced over 57,000 Tonga from their ancestral Gwembe Valley, submerging their burial grounds and homeland under Lake Kariba. Despite this, the Tonga have maintained their cultural identity and are one of Zambia's most historically studied communities (due to the displacement). They are matrilineal, egalitarian, and known for their 'bull-roarer' (ngoma buntibe) rain ceremonies.
cultural_highlights: Kariba displacement — 57,000 Tonga were removed from the Gwembe Valley in 1958 for the Kariba Dam; their story is one of Africa's most documented forced displacements
Ngoma buntibe (bull-roarer rain ceremony) — a rain-calling ceremony involving sacred musical instruments and collective prayer for agricultural rains
Bashanjo cattle ceremony — the Tonga relationship with cattle is central to identity; cattle are used in bridewealth, sacrifice, and social ceremonies
Egalitarian society — no centralised chief; authority vested in village headmen and neighbourhood councils of elders

food_traditions.name: Nshima with game
food_traditions.description: Maize nshima with dried or fresh game meat from the Zambezi Valley floor — the Valley Tonga's traditional protein source

food_traditions.name: Mukoyo (maize drink)
food_traditions.description: Sweet fermented maize drink shared with the Lozi and other southern peoples

food_traditions.name: Bream (lake fish)
food_traditions.description: Fresh and dried bream from Lake Kariba and the Zambezi — the dominant fish protein since the dam's creation

music_traditions: Ngoma buntibe ceremony — bull-roarer instruments spun above the head to produce the sound of ancestral rain spirits
Sanza thumb piano — a small lamellophone played privately for personal music and meditation
Wedding drums — specific drum patterns and songs for different phases of Tonga wedding ceremonies
